figures.gloss: A Baptist farmer-preacher of Low Hampton, New York — a deist converted after the War of 1812 — who calculated from Daniel 8:14's '2,300 days' (counted as years from the decree of 457 BCE) that Christ would return about 1843–44. His movement drew tens of thousands; Samuel Snow's 'seventh-month' refinement fixed the date at 22 October 1844. Miller himself never joined the Seventh-day Adventist Church, which organized formally at Battle Creek in 1863, fourteen years after his death.
figures.facet: Apocalypse as arithmetic: Miller's fourteen rules of interpretation treat prophecy as a solvable equation any literate farmer can work, the democratized biblicism of the Second Great Awakening pushed to its limit.
figures.provenance: William Miller, Evidence from Scripture and History of the Second Coming of Christ (1836); Ronald Numbers & Jonathan Butler (eds.), The Disappointed (1987); George Knight, Millennial Fever and the End of the World (1993).

figures.gloss: The doctrine forged out of the Great Disappointment: when 22 October 1844 passed without the Advent, Hiram Edson's cornfield conviction the next morning — elaborated by O. R. L. Crosier (Day-Star Extra, 1846) — held that the sanctuary to be cleansed was in heaven, where Christ had entered the Most Holy Place to begin an investigative judgment of the professed people of God.
figures.facet: The tradition's founding move is a reinterpretation rather than a retraction — the date was right, the event was relocated from earth to heaven, turning a failed prediction into a standing doctrine of a real temple above.
figures.provenance: O. R. L. Crosier, 'The Law of Moses,' Day-Star Extra (7 Feb 1846); Hiram Edson's fragmentary memoir; Seventh-day Adventist Fundamental Belief 24.

figures.gloss: The Portland, Maine, Millerite (1827–1915) whose visionary career began in December 1844 and ran to an estimated 2,000 visions and prophetic dreams, many received in attested physical trance. Co-founder of the church with James White and Joseph Bates, she is held to have exercised the 'Spirit of Prophecy'; The Great Controversy frames all history as the cosmic conflict between Christ and Satan.
figures.facet: A church that formally canonized a living modern prophetess while keeping her writings subordinate to Scripture — a deliberate two-tier canon; her documented literary borrowing from contemporary authors remains debated inside the church itself (Numbers; the Veltman study).
figures.provenance: Ellen G. White, The Great Controversy (1858/1888/1911) and Steps to Christ (1892); Ronald Numbers, Prophetess of Health (1976); Fred Veltman, 'The Desire of Ages Project' (1988).

figures.gloss: Saturday observance, sundown to sundown, adopted from the Seventh Day Baptists via Rachel Oakes Preston at Washington, New Hampshire (1844) and argued into the Adventist core by Joseph Bates's 1846 tract. In Adventist eschatology the Sabbath becomes the 'seal of God,' the final test of loyalty in the end-time crisis.
figures.facet: A memorial of creation transformed into an eschatological flashpoint: the tradition locates the last controversy of history not in a doctrine or a place but in which day one keeps.
figures.provenance: Joseph Bates, The Seventh Day Sabbath, a Perpetual Sign (1846); Exodus 20:8–11; Seventh-day Adventist Fundamental Belief 20.

figures.gloss: The health-reform program rooted in Ellen White's Otsego, Michigan vision (June 1863): the body as the temple of God, vegetarianism commended, alcohol and tobacco rejected. It built the Battle Creek Sanitarium under John Harvey Kellogg and today a worldwide Adventist hospital network; Loma Linda's longevity is a staple of modern epidemiology.
figures.facet: Health reform framed as preparation of the body for translation at the Second Coming — diet as eschatological refinement — which is why a millenarian sect ended up running one of the world's largest religious medical systems.
figures.provenance: Ellen White, 'Health, or How to Live' (1865) and The Ministry of Healing (1905); Ronald Numbers, Prophetess of Health (1976); the Adventist Health Studies (Loma Linda University).

figures.gloss: The schismatic line running from Victor Houteff's Shepherd's Rod reform movement (1929–30, expelled from the SDA church) to the Mount Carmel community near Waco (1935), Florence Houteff's failed 22 April 1959 prophecy, Benjamin Roden's 'Branch,' and finally Vernon Howell — David Koresh — who claimed the mantle of the Lamb able to open the Seven Seals. The ATF raid of 28 February 1993 and the fire of 19 April 1993 left seventy-six dead, Koresh among them.
figures.facet: The dark facet of institutionalized living prophecy: each schismatic claimed the same prophetic office the parent church had vested in Ellen White, and Koresh's 'New Light' doctrine (1989) used that office to annul his followers' marriages — prophetic authority overriding the community's own moral law.
figures.provenance: James Tabor & Eugene Gallagher, Why Waco? (1995); Kenneth Newport, The Branch Davidians of Waco (2006).

figures.gloss: The three proclamations of Revelation 14:6–12 — the everlasting gospel and the hour of God's judgment, the fall of 'Babylon,' and the warning against worshipping the beast and receiving its mark — read by the church as its own end-time commission, given ceremonial place on the denominational logo and in the names of its flagship publishing houses and universities.
figures.facet: The tradition's self-definition is textual before it is institutional: 'Adventist' identity is anchored to one specific three-part biblical proclamation read as addressed to this movement by name, not a general end-time expectancy shared loosely with other Christian bodies.
figures.provenance: Revelation 14:6–12; Seventh-day Adventist Fundamental Belief 8 (The Great Controversy) and 13 (Remnant and Its Mission).

figures.gloss: The retired sea captain (1792–1872) of Fair Haven, Massachusetts — a Millerite whose 1846 tract The Seventh Day Sabbath, a Perpetual Sign argued the Saturday Sabbath into the small post-Disappointment remnant's core teaching, and who first connected the Sabbath to the newly forming heavenly-sanctuary doctrine. Counted with James and Ellen White as the church's third co-founder.
figures.facet: The connector-figure whose single pamphlet welds two doctrines invented independently of each other — Edson and Crosier's sanctuary, and Rachel Oakes Preston's borrowed Sabbath — into the compound eschatology (Sabbath as end-time seal) that gives the church its name.
figures.provenance: Joseph Bates, The Seventh Day Sabbath, a Perpetual Sign (1846); George Knight, Joseph Bates: The Real Founder of Seventh-day Adventism (2004).

figures.gloss: The end-time people of Revelation 12:17, who 'keep the commandments of God and have the testimony of Jesus Christ' — Fundamental Belief 13's identification of the Seventh-day Adventist Church itself as this end-time remnant, called out from among all Christian bodies rather than set against them.
figures.facet: A claim to be the last phase of a lineage rather than something wholly new: the doctrine positions Adventism inside the historic Christian church as its final called-out remnant, not as a rival replacement — the frame inside which the Sabbath-as-seal drama plays out.
figures.provenance: Revelation 12:17; Seventh-day Adventist Fundamental Belief 13 (Remnant and Its Mission).

figures.gloss: The teaching that the soul has no independent immortal existence: the dead lie in unconscious 'sleep' until the resurrection, and the finally impenitent are annihilated in the lake of fire rather than tormented forever — a doctrine the church shares with the Jehovah's Witnesses next door, both descended from overlapping strands of the same 19th-century conditionalist movement (George Storrs foremost among them).
figures.facet: One of the family's cleanest inversions of mainstream Christian eschatology: hell as an ending rather than an enduring, and death as sleep rather than a conscious intermediate state — a doctrinal fingerprint of the whole Millerite-descended family, not unique to any one branch of it.
figures.provenance: Ellen G. White, The Great Controversy (1888/1911), chapters on the origin and snares of Satan's 'first lie'; Seventh-day Adventist Fundamental Belief 26 (Death and Resurrection); LeRoy Froom, The Conditionalist Faith of Our Fathers (1965–66).

figures.gloss: The Adventist physician (1852–1943) who directed the Battle Creek Sanitarium into a world-famous health institution, co-invented (with his brother Will) the flaked breakfast cereal that founded the Kellogg company, and was formally disfellowshipped from the church in 1907 after Ellen White and the General Conference condemned his book The Living Temple for pantheism.
figures.facet: The health message's most consequential son and its most consequential apostasy in one career — the tradition's signature health-reform empire and its first major internal schism after 1844 both run through the same man.
figures.provenance: John Harvey Kellogg, The Living Temple (1903); Ronald Numbers, Prophetess of Health (1976); Brian C. Wilson, Dr. John Harvey Kellogg and the Religion of Biologic Living (2014).

figures.gloss: The literal, visible, audible, premillennial return of Christ to earth — the event the whole movement takes its name from ('Adventist,' of the Advent) — affirmed as imminent but with no date attached since the trauma of 1844, and explicitly without a secret pre-tribulation rapture.
figures.facet: The still-awaited center the tradition orbits without daring to name a day for it again: the roster held the failed date (Miller) and its reinterpretation (the heavenly sanctuary), but not the plain hope itself that both of those doctrines exist to protect.
figures.provenance: Titus 2:13; Matthew 24; Seventh-day Adventist Fundamental Belief 25 (Second Coming of Christ).

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: Shared Millerite root — Russell's faith was rekindled by Second Adventist preaching (Wendell) and his chronology came from the Advent Christian Barbour; the Watch Tower system is a grandchild of the Great Disappointment that made the advent invisible.

GOLD SEAM: Direct descent — Miller was a licensed Baptist preacher and Millerism arose wholly inside revivalist Protestantism; his prophetic arithmetic is sola scriptura radicalized into a layman's equation.
The sanctuary doctrine is the direct answer to Miller's failed date — 22 October 1844 kept as correct, its event relocated from earth to the temple in heaven.
White's early visions endorsed the sanctuary reinterpretation, welding the scattered post-Disappointment remnant into a church.
The health message enters the tradition through White's Otsego vision of June 1863 and remains tied to her prophetic authority.
Houteff, the Rodens, and Koresh each claimed the continuing prophetic office the church institutionalized in White — the schism runs along the tradition's own live wire.
GOLD SEAM: Nineteenth-century American millenarian prophets whose date-specific hope of a transformed world survived disconfirmation by reinterpretation rather than collapse.

Manalo spent his formative years inside the American mission churches, ending with the Seventh-day Adventists (c. 1911-13), before founding the INC — the restorationist method descends through the Millerite lineage even as its conclusions diverge.

Twin cases in the scholarship of catastrophic millennialism (Wessinger): apocalyptic communities of the same decade whose end-time certainty ended in mass death — though Waco's violence arrived from outside and Aum manufactured its own.
The three angels' proclamation is precisely the commission Fundamental Belief 13 assigns to the Remnant.
The first angel's 'hour of judgment' is the investigative judgment the sanctuary doctrine locates in heaven from 1844 onward.
Bates authored the 1846 tract that argued the Sabbath into the movement's core teaching.
Bates is the figure who first connected the Sabbath to the newly forming sanctuary doctrine, welding the two into one eschatology.
Bates, James White, and Ellen White are jointly counted the church's co-founders.
Bates was a fellow Millerite before becoming the movement's Sabbath advocate after the Great Disappointment.
The Sabbath-as-seal doctrine plays out inside the Remnant's own self-concept as the end-time people who keep God's commandments.

Both churches' soul-sleep and annihilationist doctrine descend from the same 19th-century conditionalist matrix (George Storrs and the Life and Advent Union) that shaped Russell's own theology independently of Adventism, a shared inheritance rather than direct borrowing either way.
Kellogg built the Battle Creek Sanitarium that institutionalized the health message on a world scale.
The 1907 pantheism controversy and disfellowshipping is a direct clash between Kellogg and White's prophetic authority.
The Second Coming is the very event Miller's 1843–44 calculation targeted.
The sanctuary doctrine was built precisely to preserve belief in this still-future hope after the 1844 disconfirmation.
The first angel's judgment-hour message announces the approach of the event this concept names.

Two Millerite-descended reinterpretations of a disconfirmed 19th-century return-of-Christ expectation — Adventism keeps the Advent future and visible, the Witnesses relocated their 1914 expectation into an invisible, already-occurred heavenly enthronement; the same disconfirmation-and-reinterpretation move taken in opposite directions.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both root Sabbath-keeping directly in the same Exodus 20:8-11 commandment as the litmus test of covenant obedience — a shared textual source claimed by two entirely independent 19th–20th-century American movements.

Two American Restorationist traditions each codified a distinctive dietary discipline (avoid alcohol, tobacco, and for Adventists also meat) as a marker of bodily holiness, both emerging from the same 19th-century American health-reform milieu without one tradition borrowing directly from the other.
New England's 19th-c. revivalist descendants, including Millerism, grow from soil the Puritan covenant churches first tilled two centuries earlier; no direct doctrinal descent claimed.
Both are 1990s American apocalyptic groups whose leader's promise of imminent otherworldly transformation ended in the group's own mass death at its compound.

dossier.essence: Seventh-day Adventism is the Protestant denomination that rose out of the wreckage of the Millerite movement after the Great Disappointment of 22 October 1844, formally organized at Battle Creek, Michigan, in 1863 around the seventh-day Sabbath, the imminent visible Second Coming, and the prophetic ministry of Ellen G. White. With roughly twenty-two million members, most of them now in Africa and Latin America, it ranks among the most widely distributed Protestant bodies on earth.
dossier.cosmos: Adventist cosmology frames all history as the 'great controversy' between Christ and Satan, fought over the character of God's law and government. Since 1844 Christ has been conducting an investigative judgment in the heavenly sanctuary; at his visible return the righteous dead — who sleep unconscious in the grave, for Adventism denies the immortal soul — are raised and taken to heaven for the millennium, after which the wicked are annihilated rather than eternally tormented and the earth is made new.
dossier.practice: Observance centers on the Saturday Sabbath, kept sundown to sundown, with believer's baptism by immersion, tithing, and a health message — vegetarianism commended, alcohol and tobacco rejected — that built the Battle Creek Sanitarium and today one of the world's largest religious hospital, publishing, and school networks. Ellen White's writings function as authoritative 'Spirit of Prophecy' counsel, held formally subordinate to Scripture.
dossier.canon: The Protestant Bible as the sole rule of faith, with Ellen G. White's corpus — above all The Great Controversy (1858/1888/1911) — as a formally subordinate second tier of prophetic counsel.
dossier.reflects: The alchemy by which failed prophecy survives — a movement that kept its date, relocated the event from earth to heaven, and institutionalized the reinterpretation into a durable global church.
sources: Ronald L. Numbers, 'Prophetess of Health: A Study of Ellen G. White' (1976)
Ronald L. Numbers & Jonathan M. Butler (eds.), 'The Disappointed: Millerism and Millenarianism in the Nineteenth Century' (1987)
George R. Knight, 'Millennial Fever and the End of the World: A Study of Millerite Adventism' (1993)

links.cite: Ronald L. Numbers, 'Prophetess of Health: A Study of Ellen G. White' (1976)
Ronald L. Numbers & Jonathan M. Butler (eds.), 'The Disappointed: Millerism and Millenarianism in the Nineteenth Century' (1987)
